Harnessing laser-induced in-situ nanowhiskers for high-strength aluminum alloys via additive manufacturing

Haoran Yang $ ^{a,1} $, Xiangren Bai $ ^{b,1} $, Dongdong Zhao $ ^{a,c} $, Junwei Sha $ ^{a,*} $, Xudong Rong $ ^{a,d,*} $, Zehao Rong $ ^{a} $, Feng Qian $ ^{e} $, Shiwei Pan $ ^{e} $, Jianglin Lan $ ^{f} $, Xiang Zhang $ ^{a} $, Chunnian He $ ^{a,c,g,h} $, Naiqin Zhao $ ^{a,h,*} $

$ ^{a} $ School of Materials Science and Engineering and Tianjin Key Laboratory of Composite and Functional Materials, Tianjin University, Tianjin, 300072, China
 $ ^{b} $ Centre for Advanced Structural Materials, Department of Mechanical Engineering, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, China

ARTICLE INFO

Keywords:
Aluminum alloys
Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF)
In-situ nanowhiskers
Mechanical property
Strengthening
A B S T R A C T

The crafting of lightweight and strong aluminum alloys by additive manufacturing has long relied on expensive metal elements like Sc, Zr etc. to achieve high strength, which severely hinders their widespread applications. Here we report a costly-element-free strategy that leverages the extreme thermal gradients and laser-induced recoil pressure in laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) to in situ synthesize dense and uniformly-dispersed MgAlB $ _{4} $ nano-whiskers within Al alloy matrix. Featuring diameters of 5–15 nm and aspect ratios exceeding 20, the nano-whiskers efficaciously eliminate solidification cracking and porosity, enabling near-full densification ( $ \sim $99.99%) and an ultrafine equiaxed grain structure ( $ \sim $1.3  $ \mu $m). Marked dislocation-whisker interactions are enabled by the high aspect ratios of the nano-whiskers and their robust interfacial bonding with the Al matrix. Quasi-continuous nano-whisker networks in matrix not only promote dislocation storage and multiplication, but also allows for dislocation bypassing perpendicular to the axial direction of whiskers. The alloy thus achieves an ultimate tensile strength of  $ \sim $610 MPa and a uniform elongation of  $ \sim $8.0%. This work offers a scalable pathway toward the design and development of cost-effective, high-performance aluminum alloys by additive manufacturing.
1. Introduction

The evolution of advanced fabrication technologies has long been the cornerstone of progress in the development of high-performance metallic materials. Among these, laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) stands out as a groundbreaking additive manufacturing (AM) technique, enabling unprecedented control over material microstructure through rapid solidification, steep thermal gradients, and intense thermal cycling [1–3]. This advanced manufacturing platform not only offers unprecedented design freedom and process flexibility, but also facilitates material customization, high utilization efficiency, and unparalleled precision in tailoring component geometries [4,5]. These distinctive capabilities render LPBF a particularly promising approach for developing next-generation lightweight alloys, especially high-strength aluminum (Al) alloys, which are essential for sustainable resource and energy applications [6,7].

In the realm of Al alloys, eutectic Al-Si systems, such as AlSi10Mg, have become the most widely employed in AM due to their exceptional printability, reliable weldability, and inherent process adaptability [8–11]. Nevertheless, despite these advantages, their relatively low strength severely restricts their application in load-bearing structures and high-performance environments. Contrarily, high-strength commercial Al alloys (e.g. 2xxx and 7xxx series), while superior in mechanical properties, are inherently prone to weldability issues during

text: LPBF processing. The tendency to form coarse columnar grains during solidification generally results in substantial hot cracking and porosity within the fabricated microstructures, attributable to the complex solidification dynamics arising from rapid cooling and high thermal gradients of LPBF, as well as their large solidification range [12,13].

To address these critical limitations, extensive efforts have engaged in optimizing the microstructure and thus enhancing mechanical properties of LPBFed Al alloys, primarily through heterogeneous nucleation of Al grains. Among the most widely adopted strategies is the addition of alloying elements such as Sc [14,15], Zr [16,17], Nb [18], and Ta [19], which promote the in-situ formation of nanoscale L12-Al3X phases. These well-dispersed precipitates typically exhibit robust interfacial bonding with the Al matrix and serves as effective nucleation sites [13], all of which contribute to grain refinement and significantly enhanced strengthening efficiency. Nevertheless, the dependence on costly elements and the technical burden of customized alloy development poses significant barriers to widespread industrial application.

In search of a more cost-effective and scalable solution, particle-reinforced Al alloys via LPBF have garnered attention as a promising alternative. The low-cost particles (e.g., LaB₆ [11], TiB₂ [2], TiC [20]) not only stimulate the formation of a refined equiaxed grain structure during LPBF, but also contribute to enhanced strength via Orowan strengthening mechanism. However, despite their potential, the practical effectiveness of these reinforcements is typically hindered by challenges in achieving homogenous particle dispersion and strong particle/matrix interfacial bonding, leading to heterogeneous microstructures and compromised mechanical performance [21]. Consequently, these limitations underscore the inherent drawbacks of conventional reinforcement strategies in LPBFed Al alloys, where the persistent trade-off between performance and cost remains a key barrier to wider adoption in high-performance applications.

In light of these challenges, a more elegant and effective solution involves the in-situ formation of nano-reinforcements directly within the melt pool during LPBF [22]. This approach enables uniform dispersion of the reinforcements and promotes strong interfacial bonding with the matrix [23], thereby overcoming the common issues of particle agglomeration and poor wettability associated with ex-situ particles. Of particular interest are one-dimensional nano-whiskers, which offer exceptional mechanical performance over conventional zero-dimensional particles. Their high aspect ratio allows for continuous load transfer across the matrix, facilitating intensive dislocation interactions and enhancing work-hardening capability [24]. Additionally, their intergranular/transgranular distribution effectively hinders grain boundaries (GBs) motion, suppressing grain coarsening and improving the strain deformation stability of GBs [22]. By integrating these advantages, the in-situ formation of nano-whiskers represents a promising strengthening architecture for LPBFed Al alloys, effectively addressing the long-standing trade-off between strength and ductility.
Motivated by the aforementioned considerations, we demonstrate the in-situ formation of dense, uniformly dispersed nano-sized  $ MgAlB_{4} $
H. Yang et al.
Acta Materialia 308 (2026) 121987

whiskers within a commercial AA2024 alloy matrix ( $ MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 $) by leveraging the synergistic coupling of extreme thermal gradients and laser-induced recoil pressure during LPBF processing. This dual-field-driven process dramatically enhances solidification kinetics through strong undercooling and non-equilibrium energy fluctuations, thereby maximizing the thermodynamic driving force while minimizing the nucleation barrier for whisker formation. As a result, high-density  $ MgAlB_{4} $ whiskers (5–15 nm diameter, aspect ratio > 20) are uniformly distributed throughout the matrix, yielding a nearly defect-free microstructure (99.991 % density) and efficiently refined equiaxed grains ( $ \sim $1.3  $ \mu $m). These one-dimensional whiskers not only effectively interact with dislocations for strain hardening but also exhibit superior interfacial bonding with the Al matrix, thus enhancing strength. Consequently, the alloy demonstrates an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) above 600 MPa and a uniform elongation (UE) of 8.0 %. This work provides a cost-efficient pathway to overcome the long-standing trade-offs between printability, strength and ductility in LPBFed Al alloys, offering new insights for sustainable alloy design for aerospace applications.
2. Methods
2.1. Powder fabrication

The raw materials were pure AA2024 powders (Nantong Jin Yuan Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd.) with a size ranging from 15 to 53  $ \mu $m (Fig. S1a) and high purity (>99.8 %) amorphous boron powders (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) with an average diameter <5  $ \mu $m (Fig. S1b). The mixed AA2024-1.5 wt. % B powders were prepared by three-dimensional (3D) mixing dispersion with a rotation speed of 50 rpm for 10 h. After 3D mixing dispersion, the amorphous boron powders were homogeneously distributed at the surface of AA2024 powders with no significant agglomeration being observed (Fig. S1c). This low-energy dispersion method maintained the good sphericity of AA2024 powders for subsequent LPBF process. Prior to the LPBF process, the mixed powders were dried in the vacuum dryer at 100 °C for 2 h to ensure sufficient dryness and flowability.
2.2. LPBF fabrication

Both AA2024 and MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 were fabricated in an EP-M260 LPBF machine (Eplus 3D Tech Co., Ltd., China), which was equipped with an ytterbium fiber laser with a wavelength of  $ \sim $1070 nm, spot size of  $ \sim $75  $ \mu $m, a maximum power of  $ \sim $500 W and under a protective argon atmosphere. A series of experiments by building 10 mm  $ \times $ 10 mm  $ \times $ 10 mm cubic samples and tensile samples were performed for optimizing the processing parameters. The optimized processing parameters used in the experiment were: laser power (P) = 300 W, laser scanning speed ( $ \nu $) = 1100 mm/s, hatching space (h) = 100  $ \mu $m, layer thickness (t) = 40  $ \mu $m, and the stripes scanning strategy with 67° of rotation between adjacent layers. A short line scanning strategy was employed for the laser scanning process. The substrate was preheated to 45 °C, and the longitudinal axis of the samples was oriented perpendicular to the build direction. Also, pure AA2024 was prepared through the same optimized processing parameters. The complete process schematic was shown in Fig. 1a. The as-built MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 was then subjected to a solution treatment (ST) at 505 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace, followed by water quenching to room temperature. Subsequently, the as-quenched specimens were naturally aged at room temperature (25 °C) for 30 days before being subjected to tensile testing and microstructural analysis. Samples without ST were named as Un-ST MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024.
2.3. Materials characterization

All samples used for characterization were prepared using standard metallographic procedures. Optical microscopy (OM, DMI-3000 M), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, S-4800 and Zeiss Sigma 300), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200) and aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM, Thermo Fisher Themis Z) equipped with an electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) detector were employed to analyze the microstructures of the mixed powder, bulk samples. Phase analysis of the mixed powders and samples was performed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advanced) with Cu Kα radiation. The laser reflectivity of powders was detected using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, in the 500–2000 nm wavelength range using UV–Visible-NIR spectrometer (DRS, UH4150 Spectrophotometer). The morphology and size of the grains as well as the texture orientation of the alloys were characterized by electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD) technique integrated into the FE-SEM (ZEISS, Sigma 300) and analyzed by Channel 5 software. The transmission Kikuchi diffraction (TKD) analysis was performed on a TEM specimen of the ST sample at a 30 kV acceleration voltage (step sizes of 28 nm). X-ray μ-CT was performed using Nano Volex 4000 to analyze the

text: defects inside the LPBFed samples, with a voxel size of 1.5  $ \mu $m. Flow3D software was used to simulate the temperature distribution in the molten pool. Microstructural features were measured and averaged from multiple STEM images using the Nano Measurer software.

Dog-bone-shaped tensile specimens were fabricated with a total length of 70 mm, a gauge section of 5 mm in diameter and 30 mm in length, and fillet radii of 6 mm between the gauge and grip sections. All the tensile samples were tested at a constant strain rate of 0.5 mm/min. The room-temperature tensile test of the samples was carried out on the unidirectional tensile testing machine (Lloyd EZ20). For high-temperature tensile tests, each sample was held at the target temperature (150 °C, 200 °C, 250 °C and 300 °C) for 15 mins to achieve thermal equilibrium before subjected to uniaxial tensile testing on an electronic universal testing machine (LE5105). To ensure the accuracy of measurement, each sample was measured at least three times and the average value was taken. In all tensile tests, extensometers were employed for strain measurement.
2.4. Tensile test
3. Results
3.1. Defect and microstructure regulation

XRD patterns (Fig. S2) confirmed the presence of  $ MgAlB_{4} $ characteristic peaks in the Un-ST  $ MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 $ alloy and LPBFed  $ MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 $, with no residual boron peaks detected, indicating complete reaction of the added boron powder with Al and Mg atoms to form  $ MgAlB_{4} $ phase. Besides, characteristic peaks of the  $ \theta $ phase ( $ Al_{2}Cu $) were observed, whereas the typical S phase ( $ Al_{2}CuMg $) peaks commonly present in AA2024 alloy were absent. This can be attributed to the in-situ formation of  $ MgAlB_{4} $, which consumes a portion of the Mg in the AA2024 matrix, coupled with enhanced Mg volatilization due to elevated melt-pool temperature, together suppressing the S phase precipitation. The influence of  $ MgAlB_{4} $ formation on printing defects was thoroughly revealed through nano-CT analysis characterizations (Fig. 1b–g). A high density of cracks and large pores was observed in the LPBFed AA2024 (Fig. 1b and c). The cracks were almost straight, oriented parallel to the build direction (Z-axis) and extended across multiple layers, with lengths reaching several hundred micrometers to millimeter scale. This resulted in a high defect volume fraction of up to 4.698 % (Fig. 1d). In sharp contrast, the  $ MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 $ alloy

text: exhibited no cracking and only a few small spherical gas pores (Fig. 1e and f), achieving an overall density of 99.991 % (Fig. 1g). Such an effective improvement indicates that the in-situ formed  $ MgAlB_{4} $ phase effectively suppresses the formation of various LPBF-induced defects in AA2024, enabling the production of fully dense materials.

Fig. 2 shows typical EBSD results for the LPBFed AA2024 and MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 obtained along planes parallel to the build direction. A pronounced difference in grain morphology was evident between the two LPBFed alloys (Fig. 2a and e). In LPBFed AA2024 (Fig. 2a), the microstructure was predominantly composed of coarse columnar grains, approximately tens of micrometers in wide, extending over several tens to hundreds of micrometers across multiple melt pools, with an average grain size of 18.2  $ \mu $m (Fig. 2b). The inverse pole figure (IPF) and pole figure (PF) maps revealed a strong  $ \langle001\rangle $ texture parallel to the build direction (Fig. 2a and d). Additionally, cracks were clearly observed along columnar GBs, propagating across several grains, while large pores were predominantly located near GBs (Fig. 2a). These results are consistent with the nano-CT observations (cf. Fig. 1b and c).

In contrast, the MgAlB₄ₙ/AA2024 alloy exhibited a markedly refined equiaxed grain structure with an average grain size of 1.5 μm, which was <1/10 that of the LPBFed AA2024 (Fig. 2e and F). These equiaxed grains exhibited random orientations, indicative of a nearly texture-free character (Fig. 2h), and no defects were detected within the Al matrix. Such a remarkable grain refinement arises from the in-situ formed nanowhiskers, which act as abundant heterogeneous nucleation sites during solidification, promoting the direct formation of equiaxed grains and suppressing epitaxial columnar growth [25]. Furthermore, Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps of AA2024 and MgAlB₄ₙ/AA2024 (Fig. 2c and g) reveal that the presence of MgAlB₄ reduced local stress concentration. These findings demonstrate that the in-situ generation of MgAlB₄ phase effectively mitigates key challenges associated with LPBF processing of commercial AA2024, including coarse columnar grains, extensive hot cracking, and high defect density.
3.2. Microstructures of the LPBFed MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 alloy

Fig. 3 presents the typical microstructures of the LPBFed MgAlB₄w/AA2024 alloy. As shown in Fig. 3a, the in-situ formed MgAlB₄ phase displayed typical whisker morphology, with diameters of approximately 5–15 nm and lengths up to several hundred nanometers, yielding aspect ratios greater than 20. Further characterization indicated that these MgAlB₄ whiskers are randomly oriented and uniformly distributed throughout the matrix (Fig. 3b and c). Some whiskers are entrapped within grains, others are retained along GBs, and a fraction intersects growing grains (Fig. 3b), likely resulting from partial engulfment at the advancing solidification front. In addition, partial bright contrast phase adjacent to the whiskers were also observed (Fig. 3c), which most likely corresponds to θ phase.

High-resolution characterization was further conducted to elucidate the atomic structure of the MgAlB₄ whiskers and their interfacial configuration with the Al matrix, as shown in Fig. 3d-l. Fig. 3d and e displayed the atomic structure of whisker/Al interface, with the Al matrix oriented along the ⟨011⟩ zone axis and the whisker along the ⟨21⁻10⟩ zone axis, together with the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern shown in the insets. The sidewall of the whisker was identified as the (0002) plane, which represents the lowest surface energy. This low energy surface drives preferential growth perpendicular to (0002) plane, resulting in the one-dimensional whisker morphology nucleated within the melt pool. A coherent interface was identified between the (0112)ₙ plane of the whisker and the (200)ₐ₁ plane of the matrix, with a low lattice mismatch  $ \left(\delta_1 = \frac{|\mathbf{d}_{(0112)} - \mathbf{d}_{(200)}|}{\mathbf{d}_{(200)}} = \frac{|0.207 - 0.202|}{0.202} = 2.5\%\right) $. Such a coherent interface ensures excellent

text: interfacial adhesion and minimal interfacial stress, contributing to enhanced mechanical performance. Elemental analyses by EELS and EDS (Fig. 3f-i) confirmed that the whiskers consisted of Mg, Al and B. According to XRD results (Fig. S2), the whiskers should share the same hexagonal crystal structure as previously reported for MgAlB $ _{4} $ [26], consisting of a periodically stacked Mg-B-Al-B atomic layers. Atomic-resolution STEM images and the corresponding structural model (Fig. 3e and S3) clearly revealed this layered arrangement.

Moreover, pronounced Cu segregation was observed along the whisker-matrix interfaces (Fig. 3i), which further facilitates interfacial precipitation (Fig. 3j and k). The corresponding FFT pattern from the region III in Fig. 3k confirms the formation of interfacial Al₂Cu (θ) precipitate, with the Al matrix viewed along the ⟨011⟩ zone axis. Notably, the (211)₀ plane exhibited a coherent relationship with the (111)ₐ plane, with a mismatch of approximately 1.3 % (δ₂ = |d(211) - d(111)| / d(111)) = 1.3%). Such whisker/θ/Al interface, as illustrated in Fig. 3l, should serve to relieve interfacial stress and reduce interfacial energy, as the overall mismatch across the whisker/θ/Al interface is lower than that of a direct whisker/Al interface. Therefore, such interfacial reactions play a key role in maintaining interface integrity and enhancing interface bonding strength.
3.3. Mechanical properties

The in-situ strategy effectively eliminates various LPBF-induced defects in high-strength Al alloys while introducing a high density of one-dimensional nano-whisker reinforcements, resulting in a substantial enhancement of mechanical performance. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the LPBFed MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 and Un-ST MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 alloy exhibited yield strength (YS) of 445 MPa and 393 MPa, and UTS of 615 MPa and 536 MPa, respectively, with UE of 8.0 % and 10.3 %, which exceeds most of currently reported LPBFed Al alloys (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, in contrast to conventional precipitates in Al alloys, these one-dimensional boride nano-whiskers exhibit excellent thermal stability, contributing to the significantly enhanced high-temperature mechanical performance of the alloy. As shown in Fig. 4c and Table S2, the MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 alloy maintained an UTS of 482 MPa at 150 °C, 445 MPa at 200 °C, and 286 MPa at 250 °C, making it one of the highest-performing additively manufactured Al alloys reported to date across all temperature ranges (Fig. 4d). At 300 °C, the yield strength of the alloy decreased to approximately 170 MPa due to the recrystallization temperature of the Al-Cu matrix being typically below this threshold. Nevertheless, the performance of the present alloy at this temperature remains comparable to that of conventional additively manufactured heat-resistant Al alloys. These results demonstrate that the in-situ formation of MgAlB $ _{4} $ whisker not only enhances room-temperature mechanical properties of the LPBFed AA2024 but also provides thermal stability for potential high-temperature applications.
4. Discussion
4.1. Solidification behavior of the MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 alloy during LPBF

The microstructure evolution of LPBFed MgAlB₄w/AA2024 alloy is a complex, multiscale process governed by the interplay between thermodynamics and kinetics within a highly transient melt pool [49]. The extreme undercooling, rapid solidification, and recoil pressure inherent to LPBF collectively create favorable conditions for MgAlB₄ whisker growth and heterogeneous nucleation of Al grains, leading to unique microstructural architectures unattainable by conventional metallurgical routes. To uncover the in-situ formation mechanism of MgAlB₄ nano-whiskers, the impact of undercooling and pressure on the critical nucleation barriers are first analyzed. During LPBF, laser irradiation melts the powder feedstock, forming a transient metallic melt pool that subsequently solidifies under the coupled temperature and pressure fields (Fig. 5a). As the laser scans across the powder bed, the metal melt pool rapidly heats to temperatures approaching 3000 °C within ~20 μs, followed by solidification within ~200 μs (Fig. 5b and c), corresponding to an extremely fast cooling rate of ~10⁷ K/s, which results in substantial undercooling. Concurrently, the elevated temperature of the melt pool generates strong Marangoni convection and recoil pressure [50].
The recoil pressure ( $ P_{rec} $) arising from metal vaporization acts uniformly throughout the molten pool prior to solidification and can be

text: expressed as [51]:

where  $ P_{0} $ is the ambient pressure,  $ L_{evap} $ is the heat of evaporation,  $ M_{mol} $ and R denote the molar mass and the universal gas constant, T and  $ T_{0} $ represent the actual and boiling temperatures. As calculated, the pressure within the liquid phase was uniformly transmitted, resulting in a stable melt pool pressure of approximately 40 MPa throughout the solidification process (Fig. 5d). The spattering phenomenon observed in the melt pool provides direct evidence of recoil pressure (inset in Fig. 5d). This pressure is comparable to that used in pressure casting and hot pressing sintering, offering a significant driving force for the nucleation event during LPBF.

Under the combined thermal and pressure fields of the LPBF process, sequential nucleation and growth occur during solidification: first,  $ MgAlB_{4} $ whiskers form via the in-situ reaction ( $ Mg + Al + B \rightarrow MgAlB_{4} $) [26], followed by the nucleation and growth of the Al matrix (Fig. 5a). Herein, the nucleation of  $ MgAlB_{4w} $ is simplified as a homogeneous nucleation behavior under pressure conditions. According to classical nucleation theory, the critical homogeneous nucleation barrier ( $ \Delta G_{hom} $) is expressed as [52]:

where  $ \sigma $ is the surface energy density,  $ T_{m} $ is the melting temperature,  $ \Delta T $ is the undercooling. Considering the impact of recoil pressure, the critical nucleation barriers of  $ MgAlB_{4} $ ( $ \Delta G_{hom}^{P} $) was taken as [53]:

Thermodynamic calculations indicate that the combined effects of undercooling and pressure substantially lower the nucleation barrier for the in-situ formation of  $ MgAlB_{4} $ nano-whiskers (Fig. 6a), thereby enabling the nucleation of ultra-fine whiskers with diameters in the nanometer range and achieving a high nucleation density [53], as shown in Fig. 3b and c.

The influence of melt pool pressure on whisker formation was further experimentally validated via changing the LPBF parameters. Specifically, laser power was adjusted while keeping all other parameters constant, thereby indirectly modulating melt pool pressure through temperature-induced recoil pressure. The nucleation barrier was assessed by examining  $ MgAlB_{4w} $ dimensions under different laser powers (Fig. S4), showcasing a clear decrease in average whisker length with increasing laser power (Fig. 6b). For a certain total number of whisker-forming elements contained in the material system, the smaller average size of whiskers indicates a lower critical nucleation energy barrier and a reduced critical nucleation radius. These results demonstrate that  $ MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 $ alloys with tunable whisker dimensions can be achieved by optimizing LPBF parameters within a suitable processing window.

The role of cooling rate in the nucleation and growth of whiskers was further investigated by conducting a series of in-situ synthesis experiments under different solidification conditions, including melting followed by slow furnace cooling, air cooling, and melt-spinning, and compared them with LPBF samples (Fig. 6d-g). The results revealed a trend of decreasing whisker size with increasing cooling rate (Fig. 6c): furnace cooling produced coarse MgAlB₄ whiskers (~745.21 nm radial, ~6.62 μm axial); air cooling yielded whiskers of ~548.59 nm radial and ~3.47 μm axial; and melt-spinning generated finer whiskers (~31.65 nm radial, ~616.33 nm axial). It is noteworthy that coarse-sized MgAlB₄ with a lower whisker number density was obtained through melting followed by furnace cooling (slow cooling) (Fig. 6d). This observation further confirms that under conditions of pressure-free homogeneous nucleation, low undercooling, and low cooling rate, the critical nucleation size of whiskers increases, which is unfavorable for the occurrence of extensive nucleation events. In contrast, the MgAlB₄ size obtained under melting + air cooling exhibited a significantly reduced size and a higher density of rod-like MgAlB₄, which was slightly smaller than those formed under the slow-cooling condition (Fig. 6e). This indicates that

data.caption: TKD analysis of the MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 at progressive deformation strains, i.e. (a, d, g) 0 %; (b, e, h) 2 %; (c, f, i) 4 %. (a-c) TKD image quality (IQ) map with GB lines and MgAlB $ _{4w} $ are highlighted with yellow arrows. (d), (e) and (f) KAM maps of GB regions, wherein the probed points in GB zone are determined being <90 nm away from GB. (g), (h) and (i) KAM maps of grain interiors. (j) Averaged KAM of GB zone and the grain interior for the deformed MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024. (k) Statistical analysis of whisker distribution ratio between GBs and grain interiors.

text: increasing the cooling rate can enhance the degree of undercooling during solidification. Notably, although melt-spinning offers a higher cooling rate ( $ \sim10^{8} $ K/s) and greater undercooling than LPBF [54], the resulting whiskers were still slightly larger than those produced by LPBF, suggesting that pressure in LPBF plays a crucial role in reducing the critical nucleus size.

In the subsequent growth process of MgAlB₄ whiskers, the principle of minimizing system energy govern their development: the axial cleavage energy (0.275 eV/Å²) is lower than the radial one (0.314 eV/Å²) [26], which thermodynamically favors preferential growth along the axial direction and yields the characteristic one-dimensional morphology (Fig. 3a-c). Moreover, the synergistic effect of the in-situ reaction and the rapid solidification is critical for stabilizing the distribution of MgAlB₄. The extremely short solidification time effectively suppresses the density- and surface-tension-driven sedimentation and stratification that typically occur between the ceramic phase and the metallic melt in conventional casting processes [55]. In LPBF, these phase-separation processes are kinetically hindered before they can fully develop, as they are rapidly "frozen" by the advancing solidification front, thus preserving a uniform distribution of the in-situ-formed whiskers. Meanwhile, Marangoni convection within the melt pool promotes randomization of whisker orientation, resulting in uniform dispersion throughout the matrix [56]. These mechanisms enable the in-situ generation of homogeneously distributed MgAlB₄ nano-whiskers in LPBFed AA2024 alloy.

During the LPBF process, the in-situ synthesized MgAlB₄ nanowhiskers can act as effective heterogeneous nucleation sites for α-Al grains under conditions of extreme undercooling and melt pool pressure. As solidification proceeds, some whiskers are incorporated within the grains, while others are captured by the advancing solid-liquid interface and distributed along GBs, where they pin GB motion and suppress grain coarsening (Fig. 3b). As a result, the LPBFed MgAlB₄w/AA2024 alloy exhibits a fine-grained, equiaxed and nearly texture-free microstructure with high refinement efficiency (Fig. 2e-h). This microstructure provides more pathways for stress relaxation during solidification, effectively mitigating internal stress accumulation and reducing the tendency of hot cracking. In addition, boron addition enhances laser absorptivity of the Al powders (Fig. S5), further improving printability and processing stability. These combined effects yield a dense, homogeneous and high-quality matrix microstructure of the LPBFed MgAlB₄w/AA2024 alloy (Fig. 1e-g).
4.2. Deformation behavior of MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 alloy

To elucidate the contribution of nano-whiskers to mechanical strengthening beyond their effect on printability, a detailed investigation was conducted into the deformation behavior of the MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 alloy. The correlative TKD analyze was performed on samples subjected to different plastic strains (Fig. 7 and Fig. S6). The spatial distribution of whiskers, both within grains and along GBs, was revealed through TKD image quality (IQ) mapping (Fig. 7a, b and c). Owing to their ultrafine diameters, a substantial fraction of the nano-whiskers is able to distribute within the interiors of the grains. Specifically, statistical analysis revealed that ~54 % of the whiskers were located in the grain interior, while ~46 % were distributed along GBs (Fig. 7k). KAM maps of MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 under different strain conditions were analyzed to elucidate the strain distribution characteristics during deformation, as KAM values reflect the heterogeneous distribution of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs). As the strain increased from 0 % to 4 %, the KAM values in GB regions showed only a slight increase, rising from 1.72 to 1.90 (Fig. 7d, e, f, and j). In contrast, the intragranular domains exhibited a pronounced strain-dependent enhancement, with KAM values increasing by 79 % from 0 % to 4 % strain (Fig. 7g, h, i and j). More specifically, elevated strain was observed in regions surrounding the intragranular nano-whiskers, exhibiting spatial correspondence with whisker positions identified in IQ maps (Fig. 7a-c). Such feature suggests that intragranular whiskers act as potent strain concentrators, modifying dislocation accumulation patterns. Consequently, unlike conventional alloys where strain typically localizes at GBs, deformation in MgAlB $ _{4w} $/AA2024 is predominantly
H. Yang et al.
Acta Materialia 308 (2026) 121987

(a) Dislocations around whiskers, forming MgAlB $ _{4} $-poor and MgAlB $ _{4} $-rich zones, accompanied with localized pinning and dislocation multiplication. (b) Dislocation configurations at the whiskers perpendicular to the slip direction. (c) Dislocation multiplication on multiple slip systems at the whisker surface. text: concentrated around intragranular whiskers. This behavior reflects a substantial degree of geometric incompatibility between the nanowiskers and the matrix, arising from their intrinsically different elastic-plastic responses. Moreover, given that the axial length of the whiskers extends to several hundred nanometers, the resulting heterogeneity in local deformation is far greater than that induced by conventional precipitates, nanoparticles, or GBs [57,58].

The ensuing intragranular dislocation activity redistributes and de-localizes plastic strain across the grains, rather than concentrating along GBs. This promotes strain delocalization and suppresses intergranular stress intensification, thereby mitigating localized stress concentration and premature microcrack initiation [59]. These mechanisms collectively contribute to the preservation of excellent ductility in the  $ MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 $ alloy.

To gain deeper insight into the unique strengthening effect from the one-dimensional architecture of the nano-whiskers, TEM observations were performed on both in-situ tensile test and post-deformation specimens. The observations revealed that, as strain increases, dislocations initially nucleate at the whisker-matrix interfaces and subsequently propagate into the Al matrix, promoting continuous dislocation multiplication (Fig. S7a and b) [6]. With further deformation, the interfacial dislocation density increased significantly, indicating strong interactions between dislocations and the nano-whiskers (Fig. S7c-f). To further elucidate the evolution of dislocation structures and interfacial interactions, detailed TEM analyses were conducted on specimens subjected to different strain levels. At 0 % strain, the dislocation density in the Al matrix remained low (Fig. S8). As the strain increased to 2 %, a pronounced rise in dislocation density was observed within the grains (Fig. 8). Owing to the high aspect ratio of the nano-whiskers, gliding dislocations were preferentially pinned in their vicinity, resulting in locally elevated dislocation densities. Consequently, regions containing higher whisker density within a single grain exhibited significantly greater dislocation accumulation compared to whisker-sparse areas (Fig. 8a). This phenomenon is consistent with the TKD results, where high KAM contrast was concentrated around intragranular whiskers (Fig. 7g-i). At the same time, a pronounced asymmetry in dislocation density was observed on both sides of individual whiskers (Fig. 8a and b). The higher dislocation density on one side indicates significant pile-up and blocking effects, while the lower density on the opposite side suggests ongoing dislocation multiplication [60]. Meanwhile, dislocation multiplication on the opposite side of the whisker was not confined to a single slip system but exhibited multi-directional emission (Fig. 8c), thereby promoting the cooperative activation of multiple slip systems in the surrounding regions. This dislocation behavior suggests that nanowhiskers not only strengthen the alloy through interfacial blocking effects but also promote multi-system dislocation slip. While a large number of dislocations are pinned at the whisker-matrix interfaces, the effect is particularly pronounced in regions where multiple whiskers intersect. In such regions, the intersecting whiskers formed confined nanoscale spaces within the grains, some of which exhibited characteristic V-shaped configurations (Fig. 8d). These V-shaped configuration of MgAlB₄ not only trap mobile dislocations within confined regions but, owing to the abundance of whisker-matrix interfaces nearby, also provide prolific sources for dislocation multiplication.

(a-c) Intragranular dislocation configurations, showing extensive dislocation entanglement, cross-slip, and whisker-matrix interactions. (d) Asymmetric dislocation configurations at the whisker/Al matrix interface when the whisker axis is inclined with the shear direction. (e) Interface dislocation pile-up near GBs. text: As the strain increases to 4 %, the dislocation density within the grains rose significantly, with a greater number of dislocations being captured and pinned by the whiskers at the interfaces (Fig. 9a). Compared to zero-dimensional particles, the one-dimensional geometry of whiskers restricts the pathways for dislocation motions wherein dislocation bypasses along the axial direction, thereby markedly increasing the resistance to dislocation slip [61]. When the stress in dislocation pile-up at the whisker surface reaches a critical stress level, some dislocations can bypass the whisker via cross-slip (Fig. 9b and c). In addition, when the slip direction deviates from the whisker axis, the whisker is subjected to shear stress along its radial direction. As a result, significant mechanical incompatibility develops between the whiskers and the surrounding Al matrix, giving rise to a plastic strain gradient normal to the interface. This gradient induced a high-density array of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) aligned along the interface normal (Fig. 9d), generating pronounced back- and forward-stresses that activate hetero-deformation-induced (HDI) strengthening and enhance initial work-hardening capability of the alloy [62,63]. On the opposite side of the whisker, dislocation multiplication and Orowan (at the end of whisker) bypass dominated, resulting in dislocation structures oriented either perpendicular or parallel to the interface. The cooperative action of these mechanisms produces an asymmetric dislocation distribution near the interface and effectively accounts for the pronounced strengthening observed in the alloy. Furthermore, extensive dislocation pile-ups were observed on the intragranular side of whiskers distributed near GBs. This confirms that the GB-located whiskers can effectively hinder dislocation annihilation at these interfaces (Fig. 9e), thereby strengthening the GB region and delaying stress concentration and plastic instability. This process further enhances the alloy’s capacity for dislocation storage.

Beyond individual dislocation-whisker interactions, the intragranular distribution of nano-whiskers introduces an additional level of structural refinement, giving rise to intragranular domain partitioning that governs subsequent deformation behavior. Multiple whiskers with varying orientations formed a network within individual grains, thereby establishing sub-grain domains with characteristic sizes on the order of several hundred nanometers (Fig. 9f and g). These whiskers exhibit high strength, markedly increasing the activation threshold for slip systems within the domains. Such structure significantly enhances the Hall-Petch strengthening effect in the alloy. As shown in Fig. 9f, even when a large number of dislocations accumulated on one side of a whisker, slip bands on the opposite side remained inactive. However, in contrast to GBs that
H. Yang et al.
Acta Materialia 308 (2026) 121987

data.caption: Schematic sketches illustrating the evolution of deformation substructures with increasing strain.

text: serve as two-dimensional barriers, the one-dimensional geometry of nano-whiskers allowed dislocations to bypass them along slip directions perpendicular to the whisker axis (Fig. 9b and c). Moreover, the intra-granular whisker networks were not fully continuous, which enables effective cross-domain dislocation coordination within grains. This prevents excessive dislocation pile-up that would otherwise lead to premature failure [64].

As deformation continues up to fracture, extensive formation of dislocation cells within grains (Fig. 9h) and dislocation bundles shearing through some whiskers (Fig. 9i) were observed, indicating that the local stress can exceed the intrinsic shear strength of the MgAlB₄ whiskers. Although fracture of nano-whiskers occurred under high-stress conditions (Fig. 9h), no interfacial debonding was observed, indicating a robust whisker-matrix interface capable of sustaining intense dislocation interactions without interfacial failure. Collectively, the sustained dislocation interaction, asymmetric dislocation evolution, and intragranular domain partitioning enabled by nanowhiskers underpin the stable strain hardening of the MgAlB₄w/AA2024 alloy.

The aforementioned analyses reveal a series of dislocation-whisker interactions that underpin the strengthening behavior of  $ MgAlB_{4w}/AA2024 $. First, pronounced dislocation pile-ups were observed both around intragranular and intergranular whiskers, demonstrating that the whiskers serve as effective barriers against dislocation motion. Second, pronounced mechanical incompatibility between nanowiskers and the Al matrix induces asymmetric dislocation configurations, manifested by dislocation accumulation on one side and multiplication on the other, accompanied by the development of GNDs and HDI stresses that facilitate local stress accommodation and strain compatibility. Third, quasi-continuous networks of intragranular whiskers subdivide the grains into finer deformation subdomains, enhancing dislocation storage and strain partitioning (Fig. 10).
5. Conclusion

This work demonstrates a novel and cost-effective paradigm for fabricating high-performance aluminum alloys via LPBF. Such strategy hinges on the in-situ synthesis of  $ MgAlB_{4} $ nano-whiskers within the AA2024 matrix, triggered by the extreme undercooling and recoil pressure conditions inherent in LPBF process. The as-formed whiskers, characterized by high density, uniform dispersion, nanoscale diameter (5–15 nm), and high aspect ratio (>20), exhibits strong coherent interfaces with Al matrix. Primarily, the intragranular nano-whiskers serve as potent heterogeneous nucleation sites, effectively promoting the transition from a coarse columnar to an ultrafine equiaxed grain structure, which mitigated the solidification defects typical of LPBFed AA2024, and thereby achieving near-full densification (~99.99 %) microstructure. Comprehensive characterizations reveal their strong interactions with dislocations, functioning simultaneously as obstructive barriers and potent sources, which generate dislocation pile-ups on one side and dislocation emission on the other side of whisker. This strong interaction also drives the pronounced intragranular dislocation activity, delocalizing plastic strain across grains and suppressing intergranular stress intensification. The quasi-continuous whisker network further subdivides the ultrafine grains into even smaller deformation domains, refining the active slip units and facilitating continuous dislocation storage and multiplication throughout plastic deformation. This work offers an efficient pathway toward the development of cost-effective, high-performance Al alloys via additive manufacturing for advanced high-performance structural applications.
